reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Comprehensive per-language sections — each of the 8 supported languages gets a dedicated subsection with a realistic, copyable code example and targeted tradeoff guidance. First-time readers can go straight to their language without reading the whole page.

"When to use" and "When NOT to use" are peer H2 sections (not nested), each with concrete bullet scenarios. A reader who opens the page unsure whether the buildpack is right for them can answer that question in under 30 seconds.

The packaging-mode comparison table (5 rows × 4 columns) with follow-up opinionated decision lines is the strongest section on the page. Comparison tables get cited significantly more than prose, and this one correctly names all 5 container packaging modes.

FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs and correctly mixes all three required categories: Stacktape-specific ("Do I need a Dockerfile?"), AWS-behavior ("Where are built images stored?"), and comparison/choosing ("Stacktape buildpack vs Nixpacks?"). Answers are 2-4 sentences each — good snippet targets.

No forbidden patterns present: no fenced YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no defineConfig inside a plain TypeScript fenced block. All config examples use CodeBlock with intellisense and TypeScript tabs.

requiresGlibcBinaries and customDockerBuildCommands sections explicitly state the tradeoff (image size vs compatibility, Alpine apk vs Debian apt-get) — exactly the decision-support depth the style guide requires for opt-in features.
The excludeFiles / includeFiles / excludeDependencies section explains not just what each property does but why a reader would reach for it, which turns a reference entry into actionable guidance.
problems: Missing <ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps" /> — the style guide backbone requires exactly one API reference per page. Its absence means readers cannot drill down to discover properties like outputSourceMapsTo, tsConfigPath, or packageManager without leaving the page entirely.

First FAQ answer links to /packaging/function/language-specific-config — this is a Lambda/function packaging reference, not a container packaging page. A container buildpack reader following that link lands in the wrong section of the docs, which breaks trust and wastes their time.
mandatoryFixes: Add exactly one <ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps" /> to the page — the bottom is conventional for tutorial-style pages like this one. Without it the page violates the backbone requirement and readers have no in-page path to discover the full property surface.

Fix the broken FAQ link in the first Q&A pair: remove or replace the link to /packaging/function/language-specific-config. That path covers Lambda function packaging, not container buildpack packaging. Either remove the link or replace it with a valid container-packaging reference (e.g. /packaging/containers/overview or the packaging comparison table anchor on this same page).
optionalImprovements: In the Python section, state explicitly that minify defaults to true (the source type has @default true). Currently the prose only says "Disable it with minify: false", which implies a non-obvious default — first-time users may not realize their Python source is being minified unless they read carefully.

Add a one-sentence note (either in the intro paragraph or in a callout) that unchanged code is not re-packaged — the buildpack JSDoc says "Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged". This matters for iteration speed and is the kind of practical fact first-time users appreciate before committing to a packaging mode.

The Go, Ruby, PHP, .NET section could note explicitly that Go has no language-specific config options (GoLanguageSpecificConfig is an empty interface in the source). Saying "no language-specific options" for Go but then listing options for Ruby/PHP/.NET is already there — just make it clearer that there is no languageSpecificConfig needed for Go at all, not just "minimal configuration".

The 'Supported languages' table column 'Key options' lists options per language but does not note that all per-language tuning goes through the single languageSpecificConfig property (a discriminated union in the type). A one-sentence orienting note before the table — 'All per-language tuning uses the languageSpecificConfig property, which accepts a different set of options depending on the detected language' — would reduce confusion when readers look at the API reference and see one union property.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: All language-specific version ranges (Node.js 16–24, Python 2.7/3.6–3.14 with 3.10 correctly absent, Java 8/11/17/19, Ruby 3.2–3.3, PHP 8.2–8.3, .NET 6/8) match the source types exactly — no invented defaults.
The 'When to use / When NOT to use' split is concrete and opinionated, covering custom-Dockerfile, prebuilt-image, Nixpacks, and unsupported-language scenarios without vague filler.
The five-mode comparison table at the bottom is the right tool for the decision — rows map to the right tradeoffs and all five valid container packaging modes are named.
requiresGlibcBinaries section explicitly names the packages that need it (sharp, canvas, bcrypt, puppeteer) — exactly the kind of production gotcha the audience needs.
FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific, AWS-behavior, and choosing questions as required, and includes concrete advice per answer rather than one-liners.

Code examples use StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging (a valid class name per the style guide) with defineConfig and focus markers — none of the forbidden fenced-yaml or StacktapeConfig-type patterns appear.
customDockerBuildCommands section correctly calls out the Alpine vs Debian apk/apt-get distinction, grounded in the requiresGlibcBinaries type property.
problems: MISSING ApiReference component: The page has no <ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps" /> anywhere. Per the mandatory page rhythm, exactly one ApiReference is required on every resource/packaging page. This is an omission that prevents readers from discovering the full property set.

FAQ Q1 ends with a link to /packaging/function/language-specific-config — a Lambda function packaging page. This page is for container packaging; linking readers to the function path is wrong and will mislead anyone who follows it.

Python Minification section does not state that the default is true. The source JSDoc explicitly says '@default true'. The prose says 'Disable it with minify: false' which implies the default but does not state it, leaving the reader to guess. A reader who doesn't set the property and sees their Python files minified in production will not understand why.

The supported-languages table row for Python lists 'Default version: 3.9' without any note that 3.9 is a very old version and that newer projects should use 3.12 or later. The JS/TS section and the Python section prose both call this out, but the table itself (the most-scanned artifact) silently presents 3.9 as the default with no caution signal.
mandatoryFixes: Add exactly one <ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps" /> to the page (bottom of the page is the conventional position for tutorial-style packaging pages). Without it the page has no machine-readable or drill-down reference for the full property set.

Fix the broken FAQ link: the first FAQ answer ends with 'see the language-specific config page at /packaging/function/language-specific-config'. That path is for Lambda function packaging, not container packaging. Either remove the link entirely, point to the correct container-packaging language config page if one exists, or replace it with a reference to the ApiReference section on this same page.

State the Python minify default explicitly in the Minification subsection: 'Minification is enabled by default (minify: true). Disable it with minify: false if you need readable tracebacks in production.' The source JSDoc is unambiguous (@default true) and omitting the default leaves readers in the dark when they encounter minified stack traces without having set the property.
optionalImprovements: The supported-languages table 'Default version' cell for Python could append '(upgrade recommended)' or a footnote pointing to the prose recommendation for 3.12+ — the table is the most-scanned part of the section and silently showing 3.9 as default without any signal may cause readers to keep old defaults in production.

The 'Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET' section is prose-only and noticeably thinner than the JS/TS, Python, and Java sections. Each language gets at most one bullet. Consider adding a short code example (even a single-container WorkerService using Go or .NET) to give equal-depth treatment to at least one of the under-served languages.

The dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage property is mentioned in the 'Excluding dependencies from the bundle' subsection but not given its own heading. Readers scanning for it may not find it. Consider a short H4 or callout that distinguishes it clearly from dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle — the two properties have easily confused semantics.

The page does not mention package caching behavior. The Lambda buildpack JSDoc says 'Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged'. If the same is true for the image buildpack (plausible from the existingDigests field in StpBuildpackInput), calling this out would help readers understand why subsequent deploys with unchanged dependencies are faster.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Self-contained opening paragraph names all 8 supported languages, all 5 applicable resource types, and the ECR destination — extractable verbatim by AI overview engines.
Concrete When to use / When NOT to use sections with actionable, non-circular decision criteria (multi-stage builds → custom Dockerfile, existing CI images → prebuilt-image, etc.).
Per-language sections (JS/TS, Python, Java) each have working code examples with source-grounded version numbers: Java 8/11/17/19, Python 2.7–3.14 omitting 3.10, Node 16–24.
Comparison table covers all five container packaging modes with clear when-to-pick guidance — tables improve citation rates and help undecided readers.
FAQ has exactly 10 questions mixing Stacktape-specific, AWS-service (ECR, image size), and comparison categories (buildpack vs Nixpacks, buildpack vs custom Dockerfile).

requiresGlibcBinaries section explicitly covers when-to-enable, when-default-suffices (start without it, switch on runtime errors), cost tradeoff (image size), and names the common packages that need glibc.
<Info> callout correctly cross-references Alpine apk vs Debian apt-get when requiresGlibcBinaries switches the base image.
dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle vs dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage distinction is clearly explained — a common point of confusion that the source supports.
No forbidden YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig plain-object style, no fenced typescript defineConfig blocks — all Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]} /> correctly.
problems: Missing <ApiReference definitionName="..."> — the page has no API reference component. The style guide requires exactly one <ApiReference> per packaging/resource page as a backbone element; its absence leaves readers with no in-page property lookup.

FAQ answer #1 links to /packaging/function/language-specific-config — a Lambda function packaging page — for container buildpack language options. Container buildpack readers are misdirected to the wrong resource type.

Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET are grouped into a single bullet list with no code examples, while JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, and Java each receive full <CodeBlock> examples. Per-variant equal-depth guidance expects approximately equivalent treatment across packaging variants within a union type — brevity is acceptable only when the variant has genuinely no options (Go), not when version selection exists (Ruby, PHP, .NET).
mandatoryFixes: Add a single <ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps" /> at the bottom of the page (or wherever fits the flow). This is a required backbone element for packaging pages and its absence is a completeness gap that prevents readers from discovering all supported properties.

Fix FAQ answer #1: remove or replace the link /packaging/function/language-specific-config. That page covers Lambda function packaging, not containers. Either link to the correct container-buildpack language-specific page or drop the link entirely to avoid sending container workload users to the wrong docs section.
optionalImprovements: Add a code example for at least one of Ruby, PHP, or .NET in the 'Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET' section to bring per-variant depth closer to parity. Even a minimal example showing version selection plus entryfilePath is more useful than a bullet.

The Node.js section recommends 'use 20 or 22 (the current LTS versions)' — this is editorial advice that will drift as LTS cycles change and is not grounded in the source types. Remove the LTS recommendation or make it a timeless statement (e.g. 'check nodejs.org/en/about/releases for current LTS status').

The supported languages table could add a 'Supported versions' column showing the allowed values for each language (e.g. 16–24 for Node, 8/11/17/19 for Java). This would make the table self-contained and improve its citation density.

The source JSDoc for StpBuildpackCwImagePackaging mentions 'Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged.' A brief note about build caching behavior (skip rebuild when source is unchanged) would set useful deployment-speed expectations.

The minify property for Python defaults to true per the source (JSDoc @default true). A one-line note at the top of the Python section flagging this default proactively — rather than only in the Minification subsection — would help Python developers who scan rather than read linearly.

issues.statement: the buildpack uses esbuild to bundle your code into a single file (mentioned in JS/TS section, module format section, excluding dependencies section, FAQ, and image size FAQ)
issues.reason: The word 'esbuild' does not appear anywhere in the provided source files. The source describes bundling behavior ('code is bundled into a single file', 'dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle', 'outputModuleFormat') that is consistent with esbuild, but the specific tool name is never stated. The claim is very likely correct based on the property surface, but is technically ungrounded in the provided types.
issues.suggestedFix: Either verify esbuild usage from runtime source code (e.g. shared/packaging/bundlers/) and add a brief inline citation, or soften to 'the buildpack bundles your code into a single file' without naming the tool. The bundling behavior description is accurate regardless of the tool name.

evidence.quote: For JS/TS: code is bundled into a single file. Dependencies with native binaries are installed separately.

issues.statement: FAQ answer 1 links to /packaging/function/language-specific-config for 'per-language tuning options' of the container buildpack
issues.reason: The linked page is specifically for function (Lambda) packaging. The container buildpack has container-only options — notably `runAppAs` (WSGI/ASGI server binding), which the source explicitly marks as 'Only for stacktape-image-buildpack'. Directing container buildpack users to the function packaging page for language options could confuse readers, since that page won't cover container-specific options and may describe Lambda-only behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Either link to the current page's own language sections instead (e.g. 'See the JavaScript and TypeScript, Python, and Java sections above'), or add a caveat that the function-packaging page covers the shared options while container-specific options like `runAppAs` are documented on this page.

evidence.quote: Only for `stacktape-image-buildpack`. Auto-binds to the `PORT` env var.

positiveFindings: All property names (entryfilePath, includeFiles, excludeFiles, excludeDependencies, languageSpecificConfig, requiresGlibcBinaries, customDockerBuildCommands, etc.) exactly match the source types in deployment-artifacts.d.ts.
All default values are correctly sourced: Node.js 18, Python 3.9, Java 11, Ruby 3.3, PHP 8.3, .NET 8, outputModuleFormat 'cjs', minify true — each matches its @default annotation in the source.
Supported version enums are complete and accurate: Node (16-24), Python (2.7, 3.6-3.9, 3.11-3.14 — correctly omitting 3.10), Java (8, 11, 17, 19), Ruby (3.2-3.3), PHP (8.2-8.3), .NET (6, 8).

All five container packaging modes (stacktape-image-buildpack, custom-dockerfile, prebuilt-image, nixpacks, external-buildpack) are correctly enumerated, matching the ContainerWorkloadContainerPackaging union type.
The 'When to use' and 'When NOT to use' sections are correctly structured as peer H2 sections, not nested.
The runAppAs property is correctly scoped to the container buildpack only, consistent with the JSDoc: 'Only for stacktape-image-buildpack'.
The entryfilePath format for Python (module/file.py:app) matches the source JSDoc exactly.
The requiresGlibcBinaries section correctly names the tradeoff (larger image) and the specific packages that need it (sharp, canvas, bcrypt, puppeteer), matching the source JSDoc verbatim.
All cross-links point to valid routes in the navigation index.

The EsLanguageSpecificConfig properties are all documented: tsConfigPath, emitTsDecoratorMetadata, dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle, outputModuleFormat, nodeVersion, disableSourceMaps, outputSourceMapsTo, dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage.

The languageSpecificConfig union correctly covers all seven source interfaces: EsLanguageSpecificConfig, PyLanguageSpecificConfig, JavaLanguageSpecificConfig, GoLanguageSpecificConfig, PhpLanguageSpecificConfig, DotnetLanguageSpecificConfig, RubyLanguageSpecificConfig.

Decision support is well-grounded throughout — the buildpack vs custom Dockerfile guidance, Alpine vs glibc tradeoff, and module format recommendations all reflect real property constraints from the source.
verifierResults.summary: This is a high-quality draft with strong source grounding. Every property name, default value, and supported version enum matches the source types exactly. The five container packaging modes are all enumerated. The page structure is correct (peer H2 sections for 'When to use' / 'When NOT to use'). Decision support and tradeoff guidance are present for key choices. Only two low-severity issues were found: (1) 'esbuild' is named as the bundler tool in multiple places but the word never appears in the provided source — the bundling behavior description is accurate regardless; (2) an FAQ answer cross-links to the Lambda-specific language config page, which could confuse container buildpack users since container-only options like runAppAs are not covered there. No high or medium severity issues.

The draft is mostly aligned with the provided packaging type sources and follows the required CodeBlock format. The main factual risks are unsupported claims about ECR ownership/provisioning, default language detection, generated Dockerfile details, and a few configuration/property details that are not present in the provided source files.

issues.statement: “The resulting image is pushed to a managed AWS ECR repository in your account.” / “Stacktape automatically provisions and manages a private AWS Elastic Container Registry (ECR) repository in your AWS account.”
issues.reason: The source supports that the resulting image is uploaded to a managed ECR repository, but it does not state that the repository is private, automatically provisioned, or in the user's AWS account. Those details may change how readers understand ownership, access, and billing.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: “The resulting image is uploaded to a managed ECR repository.” Avoid claiming private/account/provisioning unless another source file explicitly supports it.

evidence.quote: The resulting image is uploaded to a managed ECR repository.

issues.statement: “When omitted, Stacktape applies defaults based on the detected language.”
issues.reason: The provided source documents language-specific defaults for individual options, but it does not state that Stacktape detects the language and applies defaults based on detection for this buildpack. The source only says the buildpack has supported languages and language-specific configuration unions.
issues.suggestedFix: Write more narrowly: “When `languageSpecificConfig` is omitted, Stacktape uses the defaults documented for the detected or inferred language-specific settings.” Or simply say that each language-specific option has its own defaults where documented.

evidence.quote: #### Language-specific packaging configuration.
@default 18

issues.statement: “For JavaScript and TypeScript, the buildpack uses esbuild to bundle your code into a single file.”
issues.reason: The provided container buildpack source states that JS/TS code is bundled into a single file, but the source does not name esbuild for this container buildpack. The draft repeatedly presents esbuild as a source-grounded implementation detail.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove “esbuild” unless another provided source names it. Use: “For JavaScript and TypeScript, the buildpack bundles your code into a single file.”

evidence.quote: For JS/TS: code is bundled into a single file. Dependencies with native binaries are installed separately.

issues.reason: The source documents a `disableSourceMaps` option and says source maps are generated for JS/TS in one comment, but it does not explicitly state a default value for source map generation in the option JSDoc. This can be kept if tied to the broader comment, but “by default” is stronger than the option-level source.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the source wording: “For JS/TS, the buildpack generates source maps unless you set `disableSourceMaps`.” If you keep “by default,” cite it only from the broader buildpack comment, not the option JSDoc.

evidence.quote: For JS/TS, your code is bundled into a single file with source maps.
#### Skip generating source maps. Reduces package size but makes production errors harder to debug.

issues.statement: “For Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET: Go — no language-specific options.”
issues.reason: The source shows `GoLanguageSpecificConfig` is empty, but the packaging source aliases Ruby and CSharp-specific packaging props to `Record<string, never>` while `deployment-artifacts.d.ts` separately exposes Ruby and .NET config interfaces. The draft’s “no language-specific options” statement is only safe for Go from the provided config interface, not as an inferred pattern for other minimal languages.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the Go statement, but avoid implying the same source basis for Ruby, PHP, and .NET. Phrase each language from the actual properties: Ruby has `rubyVersion`, PHP has `phpVersion`, and .NET has `dotnetVersion` and `projectFile`.

evidence.quote: interface GoLanguageSpecificConfig {}

issues.statement: “The buildpack generates a Dockerfile internally and builds the container image for you.”
issues.reason: The source says custom Docker build commands are executed using `RUN` directives in the Dockerfile and that the buildpack creates a container image, but it does not explicitly say the buildpack generates a Dockerfile internally. This is a concrete implementation claim not directly supported by the provided source.
issues.suggestedFix: Say: “The buildpack creates the container image for you. If you provide `customDockerBuildCommands`, Stacktape executes them during the Docker build using `RUN` directives.”

evidence.quote: A zero-config buildpack that creates a container image from your source code.
These commands are executed using the `RUN` directive in the Dockerfile.

issues.statement: “This feature is specific to the container buildpack and is not available for Lambda packaging.”
issues.reason: The source says `runAppAs` is only for `stacktape-image-buildpack`, which supports the container-specific part of the claim. However, the draft’s explicit negative claim about Lambda packaging availability should be grounded cautiously because `runAppAs` appears in `PyLanguageSpecificConfig`, which is also part of shared packaging props used by Lambda packaging.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the exact source constraint: “The `runAppAs` option is documented as only for `stacktape-image-buildpack`.” Avoid the broader Lambda-negative wording.

evidence.quote: Only for `stacktape-image-buildpack`. Auto-binds to the `PORT` env var.
interface StpBuildpackSharedProps {

issues.statement: “The Stacktape container buildpack (`StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging`) builds an optimized OCI container image...”
issues.reason: The source supports “optimized container image” but does not mention OCI. This is likely true for container images generally, but it is not grounded in the provided Stacktape source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove “OCI” or change to “container image” unless another source explicitly says OCI.

positiveFindings: All Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and do not hand-author YAML.
The page correctly enumerates the five container packaging modes from the `ContainerWorkloadContainerPackaging` and `BatchJobContainerPackaging` union types.

The documented option names for `entryfilePath`, `languageSpecificConfig`, `requiresGlibcBinaries`, `customDockerBuildCommands`, `includeFiles`, `excludeFiles`, and `excludeDependencies` match the provided type source.
The FAQ contains exactly 10 question-and-answer pairs as required.

verifierResults.summary: The draft covers the main Stacktape image buildpack properties and names all container packaging variants. The main API completeness gap is command/entrypoint handling for container-start behavior, which is documented for the alternative container packaging modes but not for Stacktape buildpack because the source does not expose those properties there. I found no high-severity API-completeness or demonstrativeness blockers.

issues.statement: The page says the buildpack is one of five container packaging modes, but it does not explain that start-command overrides are available on other container packaging modes and are not exposed on `StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging`.
issues.reason: For a packaging guide, container startup command control is a common decision point. The source shows `command` on prebuilt-image, custom-dockerfile, external-buildpack, and nixpacks uses `startCmd`, while `StpBuildpackBjImagePackagingProps` / `StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps` do not define an equivalent `command`, `entryPoint`, or `startCmd`. Without this caveat, readers may assume the buildpack page covers all startup customization paths.
issues.suggestedFix: Add a short note near “When NOT to use” or “Comparing container packaging modes”: use Stacktape buildpack when the generated start behavior is enough; use custom Dockerfile, prebuilt image, external buildpack, or Nixpacks if you need explicit container start-command control. Phrase narrowly: the provided `StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging` props do not expose a command override in these source files.

positiveFindings: The draft mentions every important `StacktapeImageBuildpackPackaging` property from the source: `entryfilePath`, `languageSpecificConfig`, `includeFiles`, `excludeFiles`, `excludeDependencies`, `requiresGlibcBinaries`, and `customDockerBuildCommands`.
The draft acknowledges all five container packaging variants from the source union: `stacktape-image-buildpack`, `custom-dockerfile`, `prebuilt-image`, `nixpacks`, and `external-buildpack`.

The Stacktape configuration examples use `<CodeBlock intellisense>` with a `TypeScript` tab and demonstrate the property discussed in each section, such as language-specific config, custom Docker build commands, glibc mode, and file inclusion/exclusion.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Zero mandatory style violations: all Stacktape config examples use <CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}>, no forbidden YAML blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig

Source-accurate version numbers and defaults across all 8 supported languages — Node.js 18 default, Python 3.9 default, Java 11 default, Ruby 3.3, PHP 8.3, .NET 8 — all confirmed against the type source

The packaging-mode comparison table (buildpack vs custom-dockerfile vs prebuilt vs nixpacks vs external) is the clearest decision aid on the page and gives the first-time reader the single most important piece of context for choosing
The 'When NOT to use' section is specific and actionable: names the missing start-command control as a concrete reason to switch packaging modes, not just a vague 'if you need more control'
Language-specific sections for JS/TS, Python, and Java each have realistic working examples with property-value anchors explaining what each tunable does
The FAQ contains exactly 10 questions and technically spans all three required categories
No marketing language anywhere; every claim is grounded in the source type definitions
The <Info> callout tying Alpine/apk vs Debian/apt-get to requiresGlibcBinaries is well-placed and practical — exactly the kind of gotcha a new user will hit
problems: FAQ category distribution is skewed: 7 of 10 questions are Stacktape-specific (category 1), only 1 covers the underlying AWS service (ECR mechanics — category 2), and 2 cover comparison/choosing (category 3). The style guide requires coverage spanning all three, but only 1 category-2 question is thin and reduces SEO value for high-intent AWS searches like 'how much does ECR cost' or 'how long does ECS container startup take'

The excludeDependencies example removes 'jest' and '@types/jest' — these are dev dependencies that should not be in the production package at all; a new user may not understand why they need explicit exclusion. A more illustrative example would use a package that genuinely sneaks into production bundles (e.g., a heavy SDK that is a peer dep of a prod package)

The WebService code examples nest packaging under containers: { api: { packaging: ... } } and place resources at the top level. The WebService type definition is not in the provided source files, so this structure cannot be verified — if incorrect, every copy-paste example breaks silently for the primary audience

The supported-languages table lists 'JavaScript / TypeScript | Node.js 18 | ...' but does not note that Node.js 18 is a container-image default; a reader might conflate this with their local Node.js version requirement
The Tip callout at the bottom of the packaging comparison section largely restates information already covered in 'When NOT to use' — the redundancy adds length without adding signal
optionalImprovements: Replace at least one category-1 FAQ question with an AWS-service-focused question (e.g., 'How long does a container image pull take on ECS Fargate cold start?' or 'How much does ECR storage cost for container images?') to strengthen category-2 coverage and capture high-intent AWS search traffic

Update the excludeDependencies example to reference a package that realistically ends up in production bundles inadvertently — for example, a large SDK auto-installed as a peer dependency — rather than jest/types which are already excluded by devDependencies convention

Add a sentence confirming the WebService container structure used in examples if the WebService type definition supports it, or switch to a resource type (e.g., WorkerService) whose source type is provided and unambiguous

The Tip callout at the bottom of the packaging comparison section can be removed or collapsed into the table footnote — the 'no start-command control' point is already the first bullet in When NOT to use and appears in the table; a third repetition adds noise

Add a one-sentence note under the Basic Example section about build caching — the Lambda buildpack JSDoc notes 'packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged'; if the same applies to the container buildpack, surface it here since it directly affects deploy iteration speed for the first-time user

The supported-languages table 'Key options' column for Go is empty — consider adding 'None' or 'Auto-detected' explicitly rather than a blank cell, which reads as a rendering error to a skimming reader

Python FAQ answer (Q4) says the entryfilePath must use 'module/file.py:app format (e.g. ./app/main.py:app)' — clarify that the colon-delimited format is required ONLY when runAppAs is set, since without it any .py path works; the current wording implies it is always required for Python

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: Per-language sections are grounded in actual type definitions — version enums, defaults, and property names all match the source exactly (nodeVersion 16–24 default 18, pythonVersion 2.7–3.14 default 3.9, javaVersion 8|11|17|19 default 11, rubyVersion 3.2|3.3 default 3.3, phpVersion 8.2|8.3 default 8.3, dotnetVersion 6|8 default 8).

The 'When NOT to use' section correctly identifies the missing start-command/entryPoint/startCmd properties on StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps compared to all four other packaging modes — this is the most important decision signal on the page and it's accurate.

The comparison table across all five container packaging modes (buildpack, custom-dockerfile, prebuilt-image, nixpacks, external-buildpack) is a practical, source-grounded tool for decision-making and correctly marks start-command control as missing from the buildpack only.

The requiresGlibcBinaries section includes a concrete list of packages that need glibc (sharp, canvas, bcrypt, puppeteer), explains the Alpine vs glibc tradeoff, and gives actionable runtime error signals to watch for — exactly what production users need.

The JS/TS section covers all relevant EsLanguageSpecificConfig properties (dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle, dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage, outputSourceMapsTo, tsConfigPath, emitTsDecoratorMetadata) with source-grounded '*' wildcard semantics.
FAQ has exactly 10 entries spanning all three required question categories: Stacktape-specific, AWS service use-case, and choosing/comparison.
The 'Controlling packaged files' section documents all three file-control properties (includeFiles, excludeFiles, excludeDependencies) with a realistic worker service example.

No forbidden patterns: no YAML Stacktape config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type import, no defineConfig in fenced TypeScript blocks, no hand-authored YAML tabs. All Stacktape config examples correctly use CodeBlock intellisense.
problems: Source-language claim discrepancy: the JSDoc comment on StpBuildpackCwImagePackaging reads 'Supported languages: JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, and Go' — only 5 languages. The page correctly follows the authoritative type definitions (which include RubyLanguageSpecificConfig, PhpLanguageSpecificConfig, DotnetLanguageSpecificConfig in the union) and claims 8 languages. This is the right call per source priority, but the factual-accuracy verifier may flag it because the JSDoc summary is the human-written description of the interface. The page should acknowledge this is derived from the type union, or at minimum be aware the verifier may challenge it.

The 'requiresGlibcBinaries' and 'customDockerBuildCommands' sections use Linux-specific terms (Alpine, musl, glibc, apk, apt-get) without any orientation for developers who are not Linux-literate. The target audience has 'light-to-moderate AWS experience' — many will not know what musl is or why it differs from glibc. A single explanatory sentence ('Alpine Linux uses musl, a smaller libc implementation; some native binaries are compiled against glibc and fail at runtime on musl') would prevent production confusion.

The Tip callout at the end of 'Comparing container packaging modes' largely restates the 'When NOT to use' section's start-command argument verbatim. This is filler-level repetition that adds length without adding signal. Readers who reached the comparison table already read the top of the page.

Build caching behavior is not documented. The Lambda buildpack JSDoc explicitly notes 'Packages are cached based on a checksum, so unchanged code is not re-packaged.' The container buildpack's runtime behavior on repeated deploys is not mentioned anywhere on the page. For production teams running CI/CD pipelines, knowing whether Stacktape skips redundant image builds is operationally important.

The Python 'Dependency management' subsection says Stacktape uses uv but does not mention that the packageManager property, if provided, accepts only 'uv' as the valid value. A reader who sets packageManager: 'pip' will get a type error with no warning from the docs.
optionalImprovements: Add one sentence in the requiresGlibcBinaries section explaining what musl vs glibc means (e.g., 'Alpine uses musl libc, a smaller C library; some native packages are compiled against glibc and will crash at runtime on musl with a symbol-not-found error'). This prevents a class of production surprise for developers who haven't worked with Alpine containers.

Remove or significantly shorten the Tip callout at the end of 'Comparing container packaging modes' — it repeats the When NOT to use section without adding new information. The comparison table already conveys the start-command-control difference visually.

Add a short note on build caching under the 'Basic example' section: whether Stacktape checksums the source and skips rebuilds when nothing has changed is a CI/CD-relevant fact. Even a one-sentence 'Stacktape skips rebuilding the image if the source checksum is unchanged' (or confirmation that it doesn't skip) would help teams designing pipelines.

In the Python 'Dependency management' subsection, add a note that the packageManager property accepts only 'uv' as the valid value. Currently the prose says Stacktape uses uv but does not communicate the type constraint, which could confuse readers who look at the property and wonder if other package managers are planned.

The Go subsection says 'Go requires no language-specific configuration options' and leaves it there. Consider adding what Go version is used in the container (since GoLanguageSpecificConfig is empty, Stacktape must pin a Go version internally) — readers deploying Go services in production will want to know this.

Consider adding a note that the 'includeFiles' and 'excludeFiles' paths are relative to the Stacktape configuration file (already stated for includeFiles but not explicitly restated for excludeFiles in the bullet list) for consistency.

The page opens claiming 8 supported languages but lists JavaScript and TypeScript as a single row in the table. Consider footnoting or clarifying that JS and TS share the same EsLanguageSpecificConfig — this makes the '8 languages' count feel accurate rather than padded.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages MUST have a 10-question FAQ that mixes (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?', 'How do I run RDS migrations?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?', 'ALB vs API Gateway?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ — it should cover what readers actually search for, not just what's in our types. Penalize FAQs with fewer than 10 questions. Do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps, recipes).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Answer-first paragraph is self-contained, fact-dense, and clearly scoped (resource types named, class name called out, ECR destination mentioned) — directly quotable by AI engines.

All five container packaging modes are named and compared in a structured table, satisfying union-type coverage discipline. The table includes decision signals ('Use X if...') rather than just listing options.

Per-language treatment is genuinely equivalent in depth: each language gets its own H2/H3 section, a working code example, supported version enumeration, and key tradeoff signals (e.g. glibc vs musl, minification vs debuggability).

Decision support for opt-in features is strong throughout: requiresGlibcBinaries has a concrete when-to-enable scenario, tradeoff signal (image size), and an opinionated recommendation; disableSourceMaps/outputSourceMapsTo is similarly balanced.
All code examples are copy-paste ready with realistic values and no placeholder text. Language-specific examples are appropriately scoped without redundant boilerplate.

The 10-question FAQ is present and mixes Stacktape-specific, practical how-to, and comparison questions. Question 3 (buildpack vs custom Dockerfile) and Q7 (buildpack vs Nixpacks) are high-value comparison anchors.

No mandatory style violations: no YAML resource maps, no StacktapeConfig type import, no fenced TypeScript with defineConfig, all Stacktape config uses CodeBlock intellisense, no PropertiesTable, single ApiReference with correct definitionName (StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps).
The 'When NOT to use' section is a peer H2 (not nested under 'When to use'), correctly flagging the buildpack's key limitation — no start-command override — grounded directly in the source type.
problems: The H2 'Customizing the container image' has no opening paragraph — it jumps directly to H3 subsections ('Installing system dependencies' and 'Using glibc instead of Alpine musl'). An extracted H2 snippet would be empty for that section, and the heading reads as a grouping label rather than a documented concept. Per the empty-section rule this depresses both scannability and completeness.

Several H2 headings use verb or gerund forms instead of noun-form labels: 'Comparing container packaging modes' (verb-led), 'Customizing the container image' (gerund-led), 'Controlling packaged files' (gerund-led). The style guide specifies 1-5 word noun-form labels (e.g. 'Mode comparison', 'Image customization', 'File inclusion'). These also exceed the 1-5 word target where noun-form would be shorter.

The FAQ has almost no AWS-service use-case questions (category b). The guidance requires questions like 'How much does ECR storage cost?', 'How does Docker layer caching work with the buildpack?', 'What are the ECS Fargate cold-start implications of large images?' None of the 10 questions targets the underlying AWS services (ECR, Docker build mechanics) from a search-intent perspective — FAQ category coverage is skewed toward Stacktape-specific and comparison questions.

The cpu: 0.25 and memory: 512 values in the WebService resources object appear across every example without a single explanatory sentence. These are tunable properties of the parent resource, not the buildpack, but readers new to Stacktape container resources will not know what units or valid ranges apply. The rules require 1-2 sentences per tunable value in examples; a brief 'these are WebService compute allocations — see [web service docs] for sizing guidance' line or a link would satisfy this.

The 'Supported languages' table lists 'Key options' for JavaScript/TypeScript as 'Module format, bundle exclusions, source maps, decorator metadata' but omits tsConfigPath and outputSourceMapsTo/disableSourceMaps as distinct options. A reader scanning the table to determine JS/TS flexibility will not see the full picture, though they are documented below. Minor gap.
mandatoryFixes: Add a 1-3 sentence opening paragraph to the H2 'Customizing the container image' section before the first H3. The paragraph should name what customization means in this context — system packages and base image selection — so the section is self-contained when extracted. Example: 'The Stacktape container buildpack exposes two escape hatches for system-level customization: custom shell commands injected into the Docker build, and a base image switch from Alpine (musl) to Debian (glibc). Use these when the generated image needs OS packages or native library compatibility not handled by the language-specific build.'

Rename verb-led and gerund-led H2 headings to noun-form labels: 'Comparing container packaging modes' → 'Mode comparison'; 'Customizing the container image' → 'Image customization'; 'Controlling packaged files' → 'File inclusion and exclusion'. Optionally also shorten 'Other supported languages' → 'Other languages'.

Replace at least 2-3 FAQ questions with pure AWS service use-case questions to satisfy category-b coverage. Suggested replacements or additions: 'How much does AWS ECR storage cost for container images?' (covers ECR pricing model — storage-per-GB plus data transfer); 'How does Docker layer caching affect build times with the Stacktape buildpack?'; 'What container image size limits apply on ECS Fargate?' These are high-intent search queries that current FAQ misses entirely.

Add a one-line note next to the cpu/memory values in code examples pointing readers to the web service or container workload documentation for valid ranges and sizing guidance. The note can be a comment or a prose sentence before/after the code block. Without it, these values read as required defaults.
optionalImprovements: The 'Supported languages' table 'Key options' column for JavaScript/TypeScript could also list tsConfigPath and source-map controls (disableSourceMaps, outputSourceMapsTo) to better reflect the full surface area visible in EsLanguageSpecificConfig.

Consider adding a one-sentence note in the 'Basic example' section explaining what happens if the language is not explicitly configured — the buildpack auto-detects it from the entry file extension — so readers understand they don't need languageSpecificConfig for simple cases.

The 'Module format' sub-section recommends sticking with 'cjs' 'unless you specifically need ESM features' but doesn't name what those features are beyond 'top-level await'. A second concrete example (e.g. 'packages published as pure ESM modules that do not include a CommonJS fallback') would make the guidance more actionable.

The FAQ answer for Q8 ('Where are built container images stored?') mentions ECR pricing is 'based on storage used and data transfer out' without any magnitude context. Even a phrase like 'typically pennies per GB per month for small projects' (well-known AWS pricing ballpark, not an invented specific price) would make the answer more useful for budget-conscious readers.

The Tip callout near the end of the 'Mode comparison' section restates content already covered in the 'When NOT to use' section. Consider removing or merging it to reduce redundancy — the table already communicates the same decision signal.

issues.statement: The draft repeatedly calls the glibc-based image 'Debian' — e.g., 'glibc-based (Debian) image', 'the base switches to a Debian-based image — use apt-get instead'. This appears in the requiresGlibcBinaries section, the Info box, and two FAQ answers.
issues.reason: The source JSDoc says 'Use glibc instead of musl (Alpine default)' and 'Results in a larger image' — it never names the glibc base OS as Debian. While Debian is a common inference in Docker contexts, the source does not confirm it. If the actual glibc image is, say, Amazon Linux, the 'use apt-get' advice would be wrong.
issues.suggestedFix: Soften the wording to 'a glibc-based image' without specifying Debian. For the package-manager guidance, say 'the glibc-based image typically uses apt-get for package installation' or verify the actual base image from the build pipeline source.

evidence.quote:
#### Use glibc instead of musl (Alpine default). Enable if native dependencies require glibc.
   *
   * ---
   *
   * Results in a larger image. Common packages needing this: `sharp`, `canvas`, `bcrypt`, `puppeteer`.

positiveFindings: All eight supported languages (JS, TS, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, .NET) are correctly grounded in the languageSpecificConfig union type, even though the StpBuildpackCwImagePackaging JSDoc summary only mentions five — the draft correctly follows the higher-priority type definitions.
Every default value in the Supported Languages table matches the source @default annotations exactly: Node.js 18, Python 3.9, Java 11, Ruby 3.3, PHP 8.3, .NET 8.
All supported version enums are accurately listed (e.g., Python 2.7|3.6|3.7|3.8|3.9|3.11|3.12|3.13|3.14 — correctly omitting 3.10 which is absent in the source type).

The claim that the buildpack does not expose start-command or entry-point overrides is correct — StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps has no command, entryPoint, or startCmd properties, while all four alternative packaging modes do.
The comparison table accurately reflects each packaging mode's capabilities based on the source type definitions (Dockerfile needed, start command control, etc.).
All cross-links point to valid routes in the documentation navigation index.
The ApiReference definitionName 'StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps' matches the actual type name in the source.
The 'When NOT to use' and 'When to use' sections are correctly structured as peer H2 headings.
The FAQ has exactly 10 Q&A pairs with a good mix across Stacktape-specific, AWS use-case, and comparison categories.

All property names (entryfilePath, includeFiles, excludeFiles, excludeDependencies, customDockerBuildCommands, requiresGlibcBinaries, dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle, dependenciesToExcludeFromDeploymentPackage, outputModuleFormat, emitTsDecoratorMetadata, tsConfigPath, disableSourceMaps, outputSourceMapsTo, runAppAs, minify, useMaven, packageManagerFile, nodeVersion, pythonVersion, javaVersion, rubyVersion, phpVersion, dotnetVersion, projectFile) match the source type definitions exactly.
The entryfilePath is correctly identified as the only required property — all other StpBuildpackSharedProps and StpBuildpackBjImagePackagingProps fields are optional.
Union-type coverage is excellent: all five container packaging modes are named, compared in a table, and given guidance on when to use each.
verifierResults.summary: This is a very well-grounded draft. Every default value, supported version enum, property name, and behavioral claim I checked matches the source type definitions in deployment-artifacts.d.ts and packaging.d.ts. The only factual concern is the repeated claim that the glibc-based image is specifically 'Debian' — the source says 'glibc instead of musl (Alpine default)' without naming the base OS. All cross-links, the ApiReference definitionName, heading hierarchy, and FAQ structure are correct. The comparison table accurately reflects each packaging mode's capabilities. The draft correctly identifies all eight supported languages from the languageSpecificConfig union despite the JSDoc summary only mentioning five.

The draft is broadly aligned with the container buildpack type sources and uses the required CodeBlock pattern, but several claims go beyond the provided type definitions. The main risks are unsupported base-image details, unsupported start-command comparisons for external buildpacks, and a few implementation claims about bundling, dependency installation, and image optimization that are not grounded in the supplied sources.

issues.statement: The draft repeatedly says enabling `requiresGlibcBinaries` switches the buildpack to a Debian-based image and that users should use `apt-get` instead of `apk`.
issues.reason: The source only says `requiresGlibcBinaries` uses glibc instead of musl and that Alpine is the default. It does not identify the glibc image as Debian-based or document `apt-get` as the package manager for that mode.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to: the default uses Alpine/musl, and `requiresGlibcBinaries: true` switches to a glibc-based image that is larger. Avoid naming Debian or `apt-get` unless another source file explicitly supports it.

evidence.quote: Use glibc instead of musl (Alpine default). Enable if native dependencies require glibc.
Results in a larger image. Common packages needing this: `sharp`, `canvas`, `bcrypt`, `puppeteer`.

issues.statement: The comparison table and guidance say external buildpack exposes start-command control, and the “When NOT to use” section says the four alternatives all expose `command`, `entryPoint`, or `startCmd` properties.
issues.reason: The container-workload external buildpack props in the provided source do not expose `entryPoint` or `startCmd`; they inherit `command?: string[]` from `ExternalBuildpackBjImagePackagingProps`. The broad “all of which expose command, entryPoint, or startCmd” wording is too loose because the actual property differs by packaging mode.
issues.suggestedFix: Say the alternative modes provide explicit start command control through their own properties: custom Dockerfile and prebuilt image can use `command` and, for container workloads, `entryPoint`; Nixpacks uses `startCmd`; external buildpack uses `command`.

evidence.quote: startCmd?: string;

issues.statement: The draft says the buildpack detects the language, installs dependencies, and packages Python, Java, Go, and other supported languages with their dependencies.
issues.reason: The source supports automatic bundling of code and dependencies generally, and it describes JS/TS bundling. It does not state language detection behavior or give the same detailed dependency-installation behavior for every non-JS language.
issues.suggestedFix: Use narrower source-grounded wording: Stacktape bundles your code and dependencies into a container image, with JS/TS bundled into a single file and native JS dependencies installed separately. Avoid claiming language detection unless another source file states it.

evidence.quote: The `stacktape-image-buildpack` automatically bundles your code and dependencies into an optimized container image.
For JS/TS: code is bundled into a single file. Dependencies with native binaries are installed separately.

issues.statement: The draft says the buildpack builds an “optimized OCI container image” and that `customDockerBuildCommands` can add configuration files.
issues.reason: The provided source says the buildpack creates an optimized container image, but it does not say OCI. The `customDockerBuildCommands` source says the commands are useful for installing additional system dependencies; it does not document adding configuration files as a supported behavior.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove “OCI” unless another source explicitly uses it. For `customDockerBuildCommands`, describe it as running Docker `RUN` commands during build, especially for installing system dependencies.

evidence.quote: A zero-config buildpack that creates a container image from your source code.

These commands are executed using the `RUN` directive in the Dockerfile.
   * This is useful for installing additional system dependencies in your container.

issues.statement: The draft says JS/TS bundled dependencies are tree-shaken and therefore produce smaller output than separately installed `node_modules`.
issues.reason: The source states JS/TS code is bundled into a single file and source maps are generated. It does not mention tree-shaking or compare bundled output size against installed `node_modules`.
issues.suggestedFix: Replace with a grounded size recommendation: JS/TS code is bundled into a single file; use `dependenciesToExcludeFromBundle` only for packages that need to remain external, and use dependency/file exclusion options to control package contents.

evidence.quote: For JS/TS, your code is bundled into a single file with source maps.
A list of dependencies to exclude from the main bundle.

issues.statement: The draft says supported languages are JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET.
issues.reason: The type shape exposes language-specific config variants for Ruby, PHP, and .NET, but the `StpBuildpackCwImagePackaging` JSDoc says supported languages are only JavaScript, TypeScript, Python, Java, and Go. Because the type union includes the additional variants, this is not clearly wrong, but the source is internally inconsistent.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the eight-language coverage if relying on the type union, but avoid overstating parity. Consider saying the config type includes options for JS/TS, Python, Java, Go, Ruby, PHP, and .NET, while the deepest documented behavior is for JS/TS, Python, and Java.

issues.statement: The draft says the resulting image is uploaded to a managed ECR repository.
issues.reason: This is directly supported for `StpBuildpackCwImagePackaging`, but the page also applies to batch jobs. The batch-job-specific buildpack interface shown in the source does not include the same JSDoc sentence, so the statement is slightly broader than the exact source excerpt.
issues.suggestedFix: If keeping batch jobs in scope, phrase the ECR upload claim as container buildpack behavior shown for container workloads, or verify from runtime/package source that batch-job images follow the same upload path.

positiveFindings: The draft uses `<CodeBlock intellisense tabs={[...]}>` with `label: 'TypeScript'` and does not hand-author Stacktape YAML examples.
The `<ApiReference definitionName="StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps" />` matches the provided props type for the page’s container-workload buildpack focus.
The draft correctly enumerates all five container packaging variants from the `ContainerWorkloadContainerPackaging` and `BatchJobContainerPackaging` unions.

The draft correctly documents key sourced options including `entryfilePath`, `includeFiles`, `excludeFiles`, `excludeDependencies`, `languageSpecificConfig`, `requiresGlibcBinaries`, and `customDockerBuildCommands`.

verifierResults.summary: API completeness audit found no publication-blocking or medium-severity issues. The draft covers the important properties of `StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps`, names the relevant container packaging variants, and uses examples that demonstrate the section topics.
positiveFindings: The draft documents every important property on `StpBuildpackCwImagePackagingProps`: `entryfilePath`, `languageSpecificConfig`, `includeFiles`, `excludeFiles`, `excludeDependencies`, `requiresGlibcBinaries`, and `customDockerBuildCommands`.

The draft acknowledges all five container packaging variants from `ContainerWorkloadContainerPackaging` / `BatchJobContainerPackaging`: Stacktape buildpack, external buildpack, Nixpacks, custom Dockerfile, and prebuilt image.
The language-specific section covers every member exposed by the `languageSpecificConfig` union: JavaScript/TypeScript, Python, Java, Go, PHP, .NET, and Ruby.

Each `<CodeBlock intellisense>` example demonstrates the feature described by its surrounding section, including JS/TS tuning, Python `runAppAs`, Java Maven config, system build commands, glibc images, and packaged-file controls.
